content.problemStatement:
Chef is given an integer NNN. Chef wants to construct a permutation A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] of {1,2,3,…,N}\{1, 2, 3, \ldots, N \}{1,2,3,…,N} that satisfies the following condition:


Let PiP_iPi​ denote the prefix of AAA of length iii, i.e, Pi=[A1,A2,…,Ai]P_i = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_i]Pi​=[A1​,A2​,…,Ai​]. Then, for every 1≤i<N1 \leq i \lt N1≤i<N, it must hold that median(Pi)≠median(Pi+1)\text{median}(P_i) \neq \text{median}(P_{i+1})median(Pi​)=median(Pi+1​)
Help Chef find such a permutation AAA. If multiple answers exist, you may print any of them.

**Note:** The median of an array is defined as follows:


Let AAA be an (111-indexed) array of length NNN. Let BBB be the array obtained by sorting AAA. Then median(A)\text{median}(A)median(A) is defined to be B⌈N2⌉B_{\left\lceil \frac{N}{2}\right\rceil}B⌈2N​⌉​. For example, median({3,1,2})=2\text{median}(\{3, 1, 2\}) = 2median({3,1,2})=2, and median({3,4,1,2})=2\text{median}(\{3, 4, 1, 2\}) = 2median({3,4,1,2})=2

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output the required permutation. If multiple answers are possible, you may print any of them.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases <= 1000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a permutation of {1,…,N} on a single line such that the median of every prefix differs from the median of the next prefix. Numbers are separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=2 the permutation 2 1 gives medians 2 then 1. For N=3 the algorithm produces 3 1 2 (any valid permutation is accepted; the shown output follows the construction).
Permutation 4 1 3 2 yields prefix medians 4,1,3,2 – all consecutive medians differ.

solution.approach: We construct the permutation by repeatedly taking the current largest unused number and then the current smallest unused number. Let l = 1 and r = N. While l < r we append r, then l, and move l++, r--. If N is odd, one number remains (l == r) and we append it at the end. This order guarantees that the median of the i‑th prefix alternates between a large and a small value, so consecutive medians are never equal. The construction is O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the output list (output itself), O(1) auxiliary.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        l, r = 1, n
        res = []
        while l < r:
            res.append(str(r))
            res.append(str(l))
            r -= 1
            l += 1
        if l == r:
            res.append(str(l))
        out_lines.append(' '.join(res))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
